conformant_sections.claim: Section 5.2.1 (Patch script: idempotence guard) is conformant. The implementation detects already-patched state via expected_replacement substring match BEFORE any target-file write, signals it bilingually via 'OK: already patched / OK: már javítva' diagnostics, skips the apply loop for those sites (zero bytes touched on the target), and exits 0 per the exit-code matrix.
conformant_sections.rationale: Spec §6.B.1 requires four behaviors: (1) detect that the patch is already applied before patching, (2) signal it, (3) stop with no change, (4) (implicit) exit cleanly. The implementation delivers all four: detection via site_already_patched (expected_replacement substring check) inside the pre-apply validation pass (_patcher_validation.py:69, called before any write at _patcher.py:258); bilingual signal via OK_ALREADY_PATCHED formatted per-site and emitted in both --check and --apply paths; stop + no change via the `continue` in the apply loop (_patcher_pipeline.py:173) which prevents _apply_one_in_loop / atomic write for already-patched sites; clean exit via EXIT_OK = 0. Test coverage is direct and comprehensive (test_apply_cap_only_default_idempotent, test_check_already_patched_exits_0, test_task_e_reapply_is_idempotent, test_site_already_patched_{true,false}). The plan document (04-script-1-patch.md) explicitly documents this as the design intent. No deviation or missing implementation found.

conformant_sections.claim: Section is conformant: every patch site is identified simultaneously by exact byte-level text AND 1-based line number; both signals are enforced atomically during pre-validation.
conformant_sections.rationale: SPEC §6.B.2 requires that "every patch site be identified simultaneously by exact text/pattern AND exact line number". The implementation's Anchor dataclass carries both signals (line + text), every Site entry is built with concrete numeric line constants (S1_CAP_LINE_A=688, E0_LINE=1, E1_LINE=179, etc.), the pre-validation in _patcher_validation.py enforces both signals atomically (TEXT_DRIFT if locate_anchor returns 0, LINE_DRIFT if found line != anchor.line), and unit tests explicitly cover both failure modes plus the >=8-char minimum anchor length. This is conformant on all sub-points: data model (Anchor), table (ALL_TASK_E_SITES + S1.cap fallback), enforcement (_validate_site_anchors), and test coverage.</rationale>

conformant_sections.claim: Section 5.2.3 (Patch script: all-or-nothing check gate / SPEC §6.B.3) is conformant. The implementation performs pre-validation on ALL sites in a single pass, accumulates ALL failures without short-circuiting, writes a `.patch.rejected` sidecar with the failing site_id on ANY failure, exits non-zero, and writes ZERO bytes to the target file. Apply-phase is only reached when the failures list is empty.
conformant_sections.rationale: SPEC §6.B.3 requires: (1) pre-validation phase covering ALL target sites, (2) patching proceeds only when every site passes, (3) on any failure the patch must halt. The implementation in _patcher.py:258 calls _validate_sites on all_sites before any apply call; _patcher_validation.py iterates every site and collects all failures (no early-return); _patcher.py:264 checks the failures list and short-circuits to fail_with_drift_pipeline before reaching apply_sites (line 314). The atomic-write path is in _patcher_pipeline_apply.py:82-90, gated behind successful validation. fail_with_drift writes the rejected sidecar (_patcher_apply.py:92-111) but never touches the target file. Tests explicitly cover partial-failure scenarios with byte-level sha256 pre/post assertions confirming zero writes on failure. The module docstring and AC-2.3 plan text match the spec intent verbatim.

conformant_sections.section_title: Patch script: line number handling + --force flag
conformant_sections.claim: Section 5.2.4 (§6.B.4) is conformant: line-number mismatch is treated as a no-patch signal, the `--force` flag only bypasses the line check, and text/anchor matching still applies under `--force`.
conformant_sections.rationale:
The implementation satisfies all three load-bearing requirements of §6.B.4:
(1) Text+line mismatch is reported (LINE_DRIFT failure) and does NOT cause a silent patch — _validate_site_anchors returns a failure dict which drives EXIT_DRIFT via fail_with_drift. Both signals (text via locate_anchor return 0, and line via locate_anchor return != anchor.line) are checked independently.
(2) --force only bypasses the line-number check, not the text/pattern check: validation runs unconditionally on every site before any --force filter is applied (see _patcher.py:258-263), and locate_anchor is the text matcher — its result is consumed for both TEXT_DRIFT and LINE_DRIFT classification regardless of --force.
(3) Text/pattern matching remains in effect under --force: even after --force is set, _validate_one_site still calls locate_anchor and still returns TEXT_DRIFT when the anchor is missing; the --force path only narrows the apply list to STATE_DRIFTED sites via _select_sites_for_run. Tests at tests/unit/test_patcher.py:482-497 confirm line-drift exits 2 by default; test_force_without_i_accept_line_drift_exits_5 confirms --force requires the second confirmation; test_force_retries_only_drifted_sites confirms text-matched sites are not re-applied.
An additional safety net (the --i-accept-line-drift second-confirmation gate enforced by _patcher_preflight.py:54 and gated by the TTY confirmation in _patcher_force_confirm.py) is implemented beyond the strict spec text but does not deviate from it. No deviation or missing requirement detected.

conformant_sections.section_title: Skill-creator: all Claude-specific replaced with Hermes equivalents
conformant_sections.claim: Section 5.4.4 is conformant: every Claude-specific command in the migrated skill-creator has been replaced with the Hermes equivalent, per the T3 inventory (18 rows) in MIGRATION.skill-port.md.
conformant_sections.rationale: Spec §6.D.3 requires: 'Ultra-alaposan cseréld le minden Claude-specifikus parancsot a Hermes-megfelelőjére — egyetlen se maradjon.' (Replace every Claude-specific COMMAND with the Hermes equivalent — not a single one may remain.) All 18 T3 bindings documented in MIGRATION.skill-port.md are implemented in the source code: every subprocess.run invocation uses cmd = ["hermes", ...] instead of ["claude", ...]; every env-stripping call uses hermes_subprocess_env() instead of os.environ minus CLAUDECODE / os.environ.pop('CLAUDECODE', None); the eval target path uses ~/.hermes/skills/<cat>/<target>/SKILL.md instead of .claude/commands/<target>.md; the NDJSON adapter (_hermes_event_to_anthropic) handles the Hermes -> Anthropic-shaped event translation; subagent files use agent_name registration instead of Anthropic subagent YAML; the claude.ai link has been removed; Cowork-specific sections and webbrowser open fallbacks have been removed. The remaining textual references to 'Anthropic' / 'Claude' / 'claude -p' in the migrated skill are exclusively (a) provenance/identification of the upstream source (SKILL.md line 23, migration table), (b) explicit forbidden-pattern assertions documenting what the skill MUST NOT do (SKILL.md lines 42, 52, 71; grader.md lines 5-6, 36), (c) naming the data shape that the downstream pipeline consumes after adapter translation (run_eval.py line 41, aggregate_benchmark.py line 25 — these are T3.011-contract references, not retained Claude commands), and (d) the CLAUDECODE legacy-guard strip in _subprocess.py which is a backward-compat requirement (when the parent process IS a Claude session, the migrated `hermes -p` child must un-nest). None of these constitute a Claude-specific command that needs replacement. The spec uses 'parancs' (command/invocation), not 'említés' (mention) — the spirit of §6.D.3 is fully satisfied: zero Claude command invocations exist in the migrated skill; only provenance documentation, forbidden-pattern assertions, and the data-shape naming required by the T3.011 adapter contract remain.

The plan MUST reason about the name collision.
- **Rationale**: V7 round-4 review (S5 BLOCKER) found the pre-V7 plan disabled a non-existent skill and, as written, disabled nothing. Migration correctness depends on the factory `skill-creator` being replaced, not on a fictional `openai` disable.

The plan calls it an "index-only stub" — its purpose is to be sliced for the system-prompt index, not to be a valid SKILL.md body. The promised test_frontmatter_passes_hermes_validator is missing, but the missing test is a separate drift from the body-empty design. Classification: justified design decision per §D5 with documented rationale; the missing test guard is the real defect to flag, but the deviation itself (empty body in SKILL.md.short) is intentional.

In either case they exist in src/ and violate both the rule and the strictest-linters spirit of §8.1. The noqa F401/F841 comments suppress wemake-python-styleguide (WPS201 unused-import / WPS237 redefined-builtin-style) findings rather than fixing them. This is a deviation, not a missing feature.

confirmed_findings.classification_reasoning: The SPEC §7.6 requires subagent verification but is intentionally abstract; the detailed G-verify spec lives only in plan 11. Plan 11 §116-128 explicitly defines a Phase 6 G-verify sub-agent that runs 8 specific checks (full pytest+pre-commit, Script #1 `--check` exit 0, Script #2 `--apply` exit 0, Script #3 zero-bytes-written, install --yes exit 0, sha256 byte-identical, no `skills/` subdir) against `main` after all 5 Phase-5 PRs merge — and explicitly states "Workstream G-verify is NOT in the Phase 5 DAG above; it runs as Phase 6 (separate sub-agent invocation)". No Phase-6 PR exists in `gh pr list`, no commit references Phase 6 / G-verify / integration verify, and §11:152-155 (D5) marks the decision as "Confidence: inferred" rather than verified-from-source. PR #6 (F-meta) partially substitutes by carrying the sha256 and skills/ static check as its own pre-merge gates, but this is undocumented in any PR description, commit message, or design doc — F-meta just happens to include 2 of the 8 checks as PR-merge gates, not as a post-merge integration pass on `main` with the cross-worktree Script #1/2/3 fixture runs combined. No PR description, commit message, or decision record documents WHY Phase 6 was skipped. Per classification rules, missing justification = bug.

confirmed_findings.classification_reasoning: The SPEC §8 explicitly mandates "Worktree + PR workflow kötelező" and the rule file at .claude/rules/worktree-pr-workflow.md states "FORBIDDEN: direct main commits." However, validation evidence confirms ~10+ direct commits to main (4094d2c, 9629889d, d0144ca0, 2b385ff1, 9448fa2e, e182eef, 41c5d66, 564df7f5, 26a1a5d, dc057f6b) that are NOT in any PR — these violate the rule. The CI workflow at .github/workflows/ci.yml does not differentiate push-from-merge vs direct-commit, and no GitHub branch protection exists (404 on the protection endpoint). No justifying document (PR description, design doc, migration note, or commit message) was found that waives the worktree+PR rule or accepts direct commits as intentional — the finding is purely procedural-rule violation with no documented exemption. CI PR #11 added pre-commit+pytest but explicitly did NOT add a push-source guard. The absence of technical enforcement makes the rule procedurally weak, but the existence of the rule + the evidence of multiple direct commits to main makes the deviation real and unjustified.

confirmed_findings.claim: ruff is not configured at "strictest standards": pyproject.toml [tool.ruff.lint] select = ["E", "F", "W", "I", "B", "UP"] (6 categories only). Strictest ruff config would include additional rule families (C90 complexity, S Bandit security, N naming, COM/Q, PL Pylint, RUF, ANN annotations, ASYNC, T10 debugger, etc.). The pre-commit flake8 hook uses a stricter set (E,W,F,C + extend-select B,PIE,Q,I,N,D,SIM,RSE,RET,ARG,SLF), so ruff is configured less strictly than the sister lint tool — a clear deviation from "legszigorúbb standardokkal".

confirmed_findings.classification_reasoning: The spec at SPEC.md:168 mandates `ruff + black + mypy + wemake-python-styleguide legszigorúbb standardokkal` (strictest standards), and the plans consistently repeat "strictest" (00-index.md:44, _synthesis.md:277). However, pyproject.toml:47-48 configures ruff with only `select = ["E", "F", "W", "I", "B", "UP"]` — 6 of ~30 available rule families — while the project's own flake8 pre-commit hook (which ruff ostensibly replaces/supplements) uses a much stricter set with `--select=E,W,F,C` and `--extend-select=B,PIE,Q,I,N,D,SIM,RSE,RET,ARG,SLF`. The ruff `select` was introduced in the initial phase5/B-plugin init commit (0eca979, "init worktree skeleton") with no rationale and has never been revisited in any PR description, design decision, or migration note. No justifying document was found in plans/, MIGRATION*.md, or any commit message. The counter-evidence in 10-toolchain-and-conventions.md only weakly restates that ruff is "in the toolchain" without justifying the minimal select. Per no-deviations and no-lint-silencers rules, this is a real deviation requiring fix.
